stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • A sprint cycling race is a short, high-intensity event of less than 1 kilometer.
  • An ultracycling race is a long-distance cycling event of more than 200 kilometers.
  • The Race Across the West is a one-day, 1,448-kilometer ultracycling race in the United States.
  • The Transcontinental Race is a fifteen-day, 4,000-kilometer ultracycling race across Europe.
stem: The student wants to make a generalization about ultracycling races. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Examples of ultracycling races include the 1,448-kilometer Race Across the West in the United States and the 4,000-kilometer Transcontinental Race across Europe.
While the Race Across the West is a one-day ultracycling race, the Transcontinental Race is a fifteen-day ultracycling race.
A sprint cycling race is less than 1 kilometer, but the Transcontinental Race ultracycling event, at 4,000 kilometers, is far longer.
Ultracycling races vary widely in length, from a few hundred kilometers to several thousand.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. It is the only choice that makes a generalization about ultracycling races as a category, supported by the wide range of distances shown in the notes.

Choice A lists specific examples but doesn’t generalize. Choice B contrasts two specific races. Choice C compares one ultracycling race to sprint races. None of these makes a generalization about ultracycling races as a category.
